name: CareSync
summary: A Tampa-based chronic care management platform that coordinated care for patients (mostly elderly Medicare beneficiaries) with multiple chronic conditions; it abruptly ceased operations in June 2018 after a rescue deal fell through, laying off nearly 300 employees.
lesson: Coordinating chronic/elderly care is valuable but reimbursement lags badly behind the cost of delivering the service, so burn rates must be matched to realistic payer timelines.

name: Hometeam
summary: A NY/NJ home care marketplace for hiring vetted caregivers that lost its founding CEO and president in 2018 and was forced to abandon its direct-to-consumer model, pivoting to a B2B Medicaid dual-eligible business.
lesson: A 1099-marketplace pricing advantage evaporates the moment regulators require W-2 employment, and pivoting away from the original vision often signals the first business never found true product-market fit.

name: CareZone
summary: A consumer app that helped families track an aging relative's medications, notes and contacts; Walmart bought its core technology for roughly $200M in 2020 and quietly sunset the original consumer app by 2023.
lesson: An acquisition can look like a win on paper while the product that mattered to loyal caregiving families still disappears.

name: Lantern
summary: An end-of-life and estate planning platform for families dealing with aging or dying relatives; acquired by caregiving concierge Wellthy in 2023 and fully decommissioned as a standalone product in September 2024.
lesson: Niche categories like death and estate planning often can't sustain a standalone venture-scale company and end up as a feature folded into a broader platform.

name: LifePod Solutions
summary: A Boston voice-first proactive care assistant for aging-in-place seniors that used smart speakers for check-ins, reminders and emergency alerts; the company went dark after a single funding round and is no longer active.
lesson: Voice hardware for seniors requires continuous investment in caregiver trust and household adoption that a single funding round rarely covers.

name: Papa
summary: An on-demand companionship and errands marketplace pairing college students ('Papa Pals') with seniors, sold heavily through Medicare Advantage plans; a 2023 Bloomberg investigation into abuse allegations triggered mass contract cancellations from Humana, Aetna and other major payers.
lesson: When your entire revenue model depends on a handful of insurer contracts, safety and vetting failures can unwind years of growth almost overnight.

name: Catalia Health
summary: Maker of Mabu, an AI companion robot that coached chronically ill and elderly patients through medication adherence and care plans in partnership with Pfizer and Kaiser Permanente; the company shut down after over-ordering robot hardware it couldn't sell through.
lesson: Hardware companion robots carry brutal unit economics — manufacturing commitments made on optimistic sales forecasts can sink a company even with strong pharma partnerships.

name: Pillo Health
summary: A Boston home health robot that dispensed medication, tracked adherence and connected patients (many elderly) with caregivers via facial recognition; its technology was absorbed by Stanley Black & Decker in 2020 and the product line was discontinued.
lesson: Consumer health robotics is one of the most capital-intensive categories in aging tech, and even novel, well-reviewed products often end as an acqui-hire rather than a standalone business.

name: Proteus Digital Health
summary: Maker of ingestible sensor pills that tracked medication adherence for chronic-disease and elderly patients; despite raising nearly half a billion dollars and a peak valuation over $1.5B, it filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy in 2020 after a merger collapsed.
lesson: Breakthrough medication-adherence technology still has to clear reimbursement and clinical-workflow hurdles fast enough to justify years of enormous R&D spend.

name: InnovAge
summary: The largest for-profit PACE (Program of All-Inclusive Care for the Elderly) operator; its 2021 IPO was followed within months by CMS enrollment freezes over care-quality failures, a CEO ouster, shareholder fraud suits and a stock collapse of over 80%.
lesson: Rapid for-profit scaling of a Medicare-regulated care model without matching investment in clinical quality and compliance invites regulatory sanctions that can crater a public listing.

name: Cano Health
summary: A Medicare Advantage-focused senior primary care chain that went public via SPAC at a multi-billion-dollar valuation, then collapsed under debt from aggressive clinic acquisitions and filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy in February 2025.
lesson: Debt-fueled roll-ups of senior primary care clinics can outrun the slow, capital-intensive economics of value-based Medicare reimbursement.

name: Clover Health
summary: A Medicare Advantage insurtech that used software to guide senior primary care, valued near $3.7B at its 2021 SPAC debut; the stock lost over 90% of its value amid heavy losses, a DOJ inquiry and a sharp Medicare Advantage market pullback.
lesson: Software-driven insurance underwriting doesn't repeal the actuarial realities of an aging, high-cost membership base.

name: Bright Health
summary: A tech-enabled health insurer that built a large Medicare Advantage business alongside ACA plans; after mounting losses it exited every Medicare Advantage and ACA market by 2023, effectively ending its original insurance business.
lesson: Rapid multi-state expansion into Medicare Advantage without proven claims-cost discipline can force a full retreat within a few plan years.

name: CareMax
summary: A Medicare-focused senior primary care and value-based care company operating dozens of Florida clinics; it filed Chapter 11 bankruptcy in November 2024 with $693M in debt after an ill-fated bet on Steward Health Care's Medicare business.
lesson: Taking on a struggling partner's Medicare risk contracts to fuel growth can import that partner's insolvency risk directly onto your own balance sheet.

name: GoHealth
summary: A major Medicare Advantage insurance brokerage that matched seniors to MA plans; revenue collapsed from $799M to $362M in a single year amid a DOJ kickback lawsuit and insurer pullback, forcing a Chapter 11 filing in mid-2026.
lesson: A brokerage business built entirely on insurer commissions is exposed to abrupt policy and legal shocks that can erase revenue faster than costs can be cut.

name: Call9
summary: A startup placing paramedics with telemedicine gear inside nursing homes to treat frail elderly residents on-site and avoid costly ER transfers; it shut down in June 2019 after failing to raise additional capital to scale.
lesson: Reducing ER transfers for nursing-home residents is genuinely valuable, but the staffing-heavy delivery model is expensive to scale faster than new funding rounds can support.

name: Griswold Home Care
summary: One of the largest 1099-contractor home care franchise networks in the US; it shut down all California locations in 2016 after a Department of Labor ruling required home care workers to be classified as W-2 employees.
lesson: An entire home care business model built on contractor classification can become instantly unviable in a single state the moment labor law changes.

name: Jibo
summary: A social companion robot marketed partly to combat isolation in seniors living alone; despite strong hype and heavy funding, it ceased operations in 2019 after a financing round collapsed at the last minute.
lesson: Consumer companion robots need a viable path to profitable unit economics well before the novelty and press coverage wears off.

name: Inbound Health
summary: A hospital-at-home startup spun out of Allina Health that treated complex, often elderly patients in their homes instead of hospital beds; it shut down in December 2025 after a funding round fell through amid uncertainty over renewal of the federal Acute Hospital Care at Home waiver.
lesson: Hospital-at-home economics depend entirely on a temporary federal waiver — building a venture-scale business on a policy that could lapse at any Congressional session is a fundamental risk investors eventually price in.

name: Veronica Health
summary: A value-based dementia care startup that closed before treating a single patient after its founder could not raise a follow-on round amid deep investor skepticism toward value-based care models.
lesson: Value-based care for complex conditions like dementia takes years to prove out clinically and financially — a funding window that closes before launch can end the company before it starts.

name: Current Health
summary: A remote patient monitoring platform for hospital-at-home care, heavily used for elderly post-discharge patients; Best Buy took a $475M goodwill impairment on its home-health bet and sold Current Health back to its founder in 2025 after major losses.
lesson: Even a well-funded corporate parent will divest a health-tech unit once losses outpace strategic patience, regardless of the underlying clinical promise.

name: Eclipse Senior Living
summary: A senior living operator created by REIT Ventas to manage over 100 communities; the pandemic devastated occupancy and margins so severely that Ventas transitioned all communities to other operators and shut down Eclipse's corporate operations by the end of 2021.
lesson: A new operating company built around distressed senior living assets is extremely exposed to macro shocks like a pandemic that hits occupancy across an entire portfolio at once.

name: Modern Age
summary: A longevity and healthy-aging clinic chain offering hormone therapy and wellness treatments for aging adults; it shut down in 2023 and was sold in a distressed deal after failing to reach sustainable unit economics.
lesson: Premium in-person longevity clinics face the same brutal real-estate and staffing cost structure as any medical practice, and novelty branding doesn't fix that math.

name: Quil Health
summary: A Philadelphia joint venture combining Comcast's home tech with Independence Blue Cross's healthcare expertise to build aging-in-place monitoring (Quil Assure) and caregiver coordination tools; it shut down in early 2023 after failing to find commercial traction.
lesson: Even deep-pocketed corporate joint ventures with strong distribution can fail to crack aging-in-place monitoring if the product doesn't clearly outperform simpler existing options.

name: Atria Senior Living (home care division)
summary: The licensed home care agency arm of major senior living operator Atria; it shut down its New York home care business in January 2025, laying off 161 workers to refocus entirely on core senior living communities.
lesson: Even large, well-capitalized senior living companies can find home care a margin-thin adjacency not worth the operational complexity.

name: Honor
summary: Pivoted from a 1099 caregiver marketplace to a technology and back-office platform partnering with home care agencies nationwide, then acquired the Home Instead franchise network in 2021, reaching unicorn status.
lesson: When a direct-to-consumer marketplace model breaks under regulatory pressure, becoming the technology backbone for existing incumbents can be a bigger and more durable business.

name: Care.com
summary: A large caregiver and senior care marketplace that went public, weathered background-check controversies, and was acquired by IAC in 2020 for roughly $500M.
lesson: Marketplace scale and brand trust can survive serious reputational controversies if the underlying supply-and-demand network stays intact.

name: CareLinx
summary: A national caregiver marketplace acquired by insurer Generali in 2017, then sold to health platform Sharecare for $65M in 2021, which called the deal 'tremendous' a year later.
lesson: A caregiver marketplace can find its most durable home as an embedded feature inside a larger insurance or health platform rather than as a standalone consumer brand.

name: Cera
summary: A UK-based digital-first home healthcare provider using predictive AI to flag health deterioration in home care patients; it has scaled into one of Europe's largest home care technology companies.
lesson: Combining direct care delivery with predictive analytics can create a defensible moat that pure software or pure staffing competitors lack.

name: K4Connect
summary: A senior living technology platform (FusionOS) integrating smart-home, engagement and accessibility tools for senior communities; it has continued raising capital, including an $8.9M round in 2023.
lesson: Selling integrated technology to senior living operators is a long enterprise sales cycle that rewards patient, well-funded execution over rapid consumer-style growth.

name: Independa
summary: A TV-based independent-living platform offering video chat, medication reminders and telehealth for seniors, embedded into LG smart TVs; it remains active and was recognized as a top remote-engagement company in 2024.
lesson: Distribution partnerships with consumer electronics makers can give a niche senior-tech platform reach far beyond what direct-to-consumer sales could achieve.

name: PointClickCare
summary: A dominant cloud-based EHR and care coordination platform for long-term and post-acute care facilities, now one of the largest senior care software companies in North America.
lesson: Owning the system-of-record for long-term care facilities creates deep switching costs and a durable enterprise franchise.

name: CarePredict
summary: A wearable and AI platform detecting behavior changes and health risks in senior living residents, still operating with a lean, capital-efficient team over a decade after founding.
lesson: A tightly-scoped wearable-plus-AI product for a defined enterprise buyer (senior living operators) can sustain a long, durable runway on modest funding.

name: Reflexion Health
summary: Maker of the FDA-cleared VERA virtual physical therapy assistant used heavily by older patients recovering from joint surgery; it remains active more than a decade after founding.
lesson: Clearing FDA regulatory hurdles early can be the difference between a virtual therapy product that survives reimbursement scrutiny and one that doesn't.

name: SafelyYou
summary: An AI video system that detects and analyzes falls in memory care and dementia care settings, now deployed across a large share of the US senior living market.
lesson: Solving a high-stakes, high-liability problem like falls in dementia care with clinically validated AI can command strong enterprise adoption despite a crowded fall-detection market.

name: VirtuSense
summary: A fall-prevention sensor platform that predicts fall risk in senior living and hospital settings before a fall occurs, still operating and expanding deployments over a decade in.
lesson: Predictive fall-prevention has proven a more durable value proposition for facility buyers than reactive fall-detection alone.

name: Sensi.AI
summary: An AI audio-monitoring platform that detects distress, falls and health changes in home care settings without cameras, raising a $45M Series C to expand deployment.
lesson: Privacy-conscious, audio-only monitoring can win trust with families and agencies that reject camera-based alternatives.

name: Aloe Care Health
summary: A connected home safety system for aging-in-place seniors combining a smart hub, voice alerts and a family caregiving app; it has continued to raise funding and expand distribution.
lesson: Bundling emergency response, ambient sensing and family notifications into one product answers more of a caregiver's needs than single-purpose PERS devices.

name: ElliQ (Intuition Robotics)
summary: A proactive AI companion device for isolated older adults; New York State's Office for the Aging purchased roughly 800 units, and a large majority of users reported reduced loneliness after a year.
lesson: Government aging agencies can be a powerful, underused distribution channel and validation source for companion technology.

name: ianacare
summary: A caregiver support platform coordinating help networks and employer caregiving benefits for people caring for aging family members; it remains active in the employer-benefits caregiving niche.
lesson: Selling caregiver support as an employee benefit gives a niche platform a scalable, recurring enterprise revenue channel.

name: Wellthy
summary: A caregiving concierge platform helping employees manage care for aging parents and family members; it has grown through acquisitions of Torchlight (2023) and Lantern's end-of-life planning assets (2023).
lesson: Consolidating adjacent caregiving-benefit products under one platform lets survivors absorb the customer base of failed or acquired niche competitors.

name: Torchlight
summary: An employer-sponsored caregiving benefits platform for employees supporting aging or disabled family members, acquired by Wellthy in 2023 to expand its enterprise caregiving footprint.
lesson: An employer-benefits caregiving platform can achieve a clean exit by consolidating into a larger caregiving concierge rather than competing head-on.

name: Trualta
summary: A training and education platform for family and professional caregivers of aging adults, used by health plans and home care agencies; it remains active in the caregiver-training niche.
lesson: Training content for unpaid family caregivers is a defensible niche when sold through health plans rather than directly to consumers.

name: CareAcademy
summary: An online training platform for professional caregivers serving home care agencies nationwide, still operating and widely used for state-mandated caregiver certification.
lesson: Compliance-driven training requirements create a recurring, sticky enterprise revenue base for agency-facing education platforms.

name: Cariloop
summary: A caregiving support platform pairing employees with dedicated 'Care Coaches' to help navigate care for aging parents, sold through employer benefits; it remains active and continues to raise funding.
lesson: Combining human care coaches with software gives caregiving-benefit platforms a differentiated, harder-to-copy service layer.

name: Homethrive
summary: An employer-benefits caregiving platform offering care concierge services and an AI caregiving assistant for employees supporting aging relatives; it continues to expand its enterprise client base.
lesson: Entering the employer caregiving-benefits category after early pioneers proved demand let later entrants scale faster with less market education needed.

name: Seniorlink / Careforth
summary: Built the Vela caregiver coordination app and the Caregiver Homes structured family caregiving program; consolidated all brands under the Careforth name in 2023 and remains one of the largest structured family caregiving companies.
lesson: A long-running caregiving company can reinvent its brand and technology stack repeatedly without losing its underlying payer relationships.

name: Vynca
summary: An advance care planning software platform used by health systems to document and share end-of-life and palliative care wishes for seriously ill and elderly patients; it remains active nationally.
lesson: Digitizing advance care planning solves a genuine interoperability problem for health systems that paper-based POLST forms could not.

name: Koda Health
summary: An advance care planning platform helping patients and families document end-of-life wishes digitally, raising venture funding to expand into health system partnerships.
lesson: Advance care planning tech launched post-COVID benefited from a heightened cultural focus on end-of-life preparedness.

name: Forcura
summary: A care coordination and referral management software platform for home health and hospice providers, still widely used across the post-acute care industry.
lesson: Back-office referral and documentation workflows for hospice and home health are unglamorous but durable enterprise software niches.

name: Trella Health
summary: A data analytics platform helping home health and hospice providers benchmark referral and performance data; it was acquired by Machinify in 2024 after building a strong post-acute analytics niche.
lesson: Analytics that help hospice and home health providers win referrals in a fragmented market can become an attractive acquisition target for larger health-data platforms.

name: Homecare Homebase
summary: A dominant point-of-care software platform for home health and hospice agencies, long considered a market leader in post-acute care technology.
lesson: Being first to build the operating system for an entire care setting (hospice/home health) creates lasting incumbency advantages.

name: Aspire Health
summary: An in-home palliative care company serving seriously ill, often elderly patients; acquired by insurer Anthem in 2018 to build out its palliative care network.
lesson: Payers see in-home palliative care as a direct lever to cut costly late-stage hospitalizations, making it an attractive acquisition category.

name: Life Alert
summary: The original 'I've fallen and I can't get up' personal emergency response system brand; it remains a privately held, profitable market leader in medical alert devices nearly four decades later.
lesson: A single, deeply memorable brand promise can sustain category leadership for decades without needing venture funding.

name: Medical Guardian
summary: A leading medical alert / PERS company offering in-home and mobile devices for seniors, grown into one of the largest players in the category under private equity ownership.
lesson: The PERS category has proven durable and consolidatable, rewarding operators who can scale distribution and customer service.

name: GreatCall / Lively
summary: Maker of senior-friendly cell phones and medical alert services (Jitterbug/Lively); Best Buy acquired it for $800M in 2018, and it remains one of the few parts of Best Buy Health's aging-in-place business considered a durable success.
lesson: Purpose-built devices for seniors' actual usability needs can command a premium acquisition price even inside a large retailer's broader, more mixed health strategy.

name: GrandPad
summary: A senior-friendly tablet designed for video calls, photo sharing and simplified apps to reduce isolation in older adults; acquired by Synchronoss Technologies in 2023.
lesson: Designing hardware around radical simplicity for a specific underserved user (isolated seniors) can create a defensible product category.

name: Devoted Health
summary: A tech-enabled Medicare Advantage insurer built from the ground up with in-house clinical and software tools, one of the most heavily funded senior-focused startups ever, still growing rapidly as a private company.
lesson: Massive capital deployed patiently into building both insurance infrastructure and clinical care from scratch can outcompete incumbents in Medicare Advantage.

name: Oak Street Health
summary: A value-based primary care chain built specifically for Medicare patients; CVS Health acquired it for $10.6B in 2023 to anchor its senior primary care strategy.
lesson: Purpose-built senior primary care clinics with a value-based reimbursement model became one of the most sought-after acquisition targets in healthcare.

name: Iora Health
summary: A Medicare-focused, relationship-driven primary care model for seniors; acquired by One Medical in 2021 in a deal announced at $2.1B to expand into the senior care market.
lesson: A differentiated senior care delivery model can command a premium acquisition price from companies seeking to expand beyond younger, commercially-insured populations.

name: naviHealth
summary: A post-acute care coordination platform helping health plans manage seniors' transitions out of hospitals; passed from Cardinal Health to private equity to Optum/UnitedHealth in a deal reportedly worth over $1 billion.
lesson: Post-acute care coordination software for Medicare populations can command repeated premium valuations as ownership changes hands.

name: CarePort Health
summary: A care coordination platform connecting acute and post-acute providers for elderly and complex patients; acquired by WellSky from Allscripts for $1.35B in 2020.
lesson: Connecting hospitals to the fragmented post-acute care ecosystem (much of it serving seniors) creates a defensible network-effects business.

name: Contessa Health
summary: A hospital-at-home and skilled-nursing-facility-at-home startup serving high-acuity, largely elderly patients; acquired by Amedisys for roughly $240.7M in 2021.
lesson: Hospital-at-home models proven with strong claims analytics can become attractive acquisition targets for large home health companies looking to move up the acuity curve.

name: Landmark Health
summary: An in-home medical care company for the sickest, frailest senior populations, providing physician-led home visits across 17 states; acquired by Optum/UnitedHealth for approximately $3.5B in 2021.
lesson: Focusing on the highest-need, highest-cost senior subpopulation creates outsized value for payers looking to reduce total cost of care.

name: WelbeHealth
summary: A PACE (Program of All-Inclusive Care for the Elderly) provider offering comprehensive senior care to keep frail seniors out of nursing homes; it has expanded steadily across California and beyond through multiple funding rounds.
lesson: Disciplined, staged expansion of a heavily regulated Medicare/Medicaid PACE model can avoid the compliance blowups that hit faster-scaling competitors like InnovAge.

name: True Link Financial
summary: A financial monitoring and fraud-protection platform designed to protect seniors and vulnerable adults from financial exploitation, still operating and used by professional fiduciaries and families.
lesson: Elder financial fraud protection is a durable niche once trust is established with professional fiduciaries and family caregivers.

name: DUOS
summary: A senior care navigation platform pairing older adults with a dedicated 'Duo' who arranges rides, meals, home repairs and medical care coordination; it raised a $130M growth round in 2025 to scale through Medicare Advantage plans.
lesson: Bundling everyday concierge support with healthcare navigation and selling through Medicare Advantage plans creates a scalable payer-funded distribution channel.

name: Cake
summary: A free end-of-life planning platform helping people (often adult children of aging parents) document care wishes, wills and legacy plans, sustained through healthcare partnerships and grants.
lesson: A free, mission-driven end-of-life planning tool can sustain itself for years by partnering with hospitals and nonprofits rather than chasing a pure consumer-subscription model.

name: Empathy
summary: A bereavement and estate-settlement platform helping families navigate the aftermath of a parent or relative's death, backed by major investors and growing quickly through employer and insurer partnerships.
lesson: The unmet administrative burden facing adult children after a parent's death proved to be a large enough problem to attract major venture backing.

name: PillPack
summary: A pharmacy that pre-sorted medications by dose and time, heavily used by seniors managing multiple prescriptions; acquired by Amazon for $753M in 2018, a landmark exit in pharmacy tech.
lesson: Solving the tedious, error-prone problem of multi-medication management for chronic/elderly patients can become a category-defining acquisition target.

name: A Place for Mom
summary: The largest senior living referral marketplace in the US, matching families with assisted living and memory care communities; it has scaled into a dominant, highly profitable lead-generation business over two decades.
lesson: Owning the top-of-funnel search experience for a fragmented, high-stakes purchase decision (choosing senior living) builds a durable marketplace moat.
